33	# Effort Breaker
34	
35	You take **one high-effort blueprint declaration** and split it into a `\uses{}`-linked chain of **smaller, easier sub-lemmas**, so the prover formalizes a sequence of small goals instead of one large one. The target's proof becomes "by `L1`, `L2`, …, `L_n`", each `L_i` is a new block with its own statement, informal proof, and `\uses{}`, and each `L_i` is strictly easier than the original.
36	
37	You decompose DOWN (one node → many smaller ones). This is the complement of the dag-walker (which fills the cone UP). You do not invent new mathematics — you re-express the target's existing proof as a chain of named intermediate results.
38

65	```

67	## How you break
68	
69	1. Read the target block on disk and inspect it: `archon dag-query node --node <target> --json` (effort, deps, used-by). `archon` is on PATH; `--json` is parseable (banner on stderr).
70	2. Read the target's informal proof (and the source proof it cites, per the directive's references). Identify the **steps** — each self-contained claim the proof establishes on the way to the conclusion.
71	3. For each step, author a new sub-lemma block in the appropriate chapter:
72	   - a `\label{lem:<descriptive>}` and a `\lean{...}` hint (the Lean name the prover will use; name it by convention if the directive doesn't give one, and note it for the plan agent),
73	   - the **statement** of that step in the project's notation,
74	   - an informal `\begin{proof}` for the step with `\uses{...}` naming its own dependencies (existing blocks, Mathlib anchors, or earlier sub-lemmas in this chain),
75	   - citation discipline (below) when the step derives from a source.
76	4. **Rewrite the target's proof** to invoke the chain: a short proof body that combines `L1 … L_n`, with `\uses{lem:L1, …, lem:L_n}` (plus any direct deps that remain). The target keeps its statement and `\lean{}` unchanged.
77	5. Each `L_i` must be **strictly smaller** than the target (smaller proof, fewer moving parts). If a step is still large, either break it into sub-steps now (at fine granularity) or flag it in the report so the dispatcher can re-break it next round.
78	
79	Granularity is the directive's call. "One level" = the proof's main steps. "Fine" = one mathematical assertion per lemma — the limit is the same as the fine-grained prover: a lemma so small its proof is a single move. There is no floor; if a break didn't help, the next dispatch breaks further.

84	- **`\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor** — when a step is a result Mathlib already provides, write it as an anchor (`\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}`, `\mathlibok`, `\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}`, no proof) rather than re-proving it. Mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote — a wrong anchor is worse than leaving the step to prove.
85	- **Citation discipline (hard rule):** every block derived from a source needs `% SOURCE:` (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a verbatim original-language `% SOURCE QUOTE:` copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` first line. Never write these from memory; if you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (Bash, blocking — needs `references/**` in your write-domain), wait, read the new file, then write the cited block. Never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.
86	
87	## Rules
88	
89	- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). No `.lean`, no `content.tex`, no `macros/`. A needed macro is a "Notes" item.
90	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\begin`/`\end`, balanced braces).
91	- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no project-history narrative.
92	- **Preserve the target's statement and `\lean{}`.** You re-express its *proof* as a chain; you do not weaken or restate the theorem. (If the target's statement looks wrong, do NOT silently fix it — flag it under "Notes" as a strategy item.)
93	- **Conserve the mathematics.** The chain must actually prove the target — every gap the original proof crossed is covered by some `L_i`. Don't drop a hard step by relabelling it; a step you cannot decompose or prove is reported, not hidden.
94	- **Verify with the graph.** After breaking, run `archon dag-query node --node <target>` and `archon dag-query ancestors --node <target>`: the target's effort should drop, the new `L_i` should appear, and there must be no broken `\uses{}`. Fix and re-check.

11	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
12	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
13	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
14	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
15	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
16	out of scope here.
17	
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
19	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
21	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
22	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
23	\[
24	  \begin{array}{ccc}
25	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
26	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
27	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
28	  \end{array}
29	\]
30	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
31	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
32	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
33	
34	\section{The canonical base-change map}
35	
36	\begin{definition}\leanok
37	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
38	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
39	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
40	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
41	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
42	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
43	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
44	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
45	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
46	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
47	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
48	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
49	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
50	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
51	  change, I.}
52	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
53	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
54	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
55	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
56	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
57	  \[
58	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
59	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
60	  \]
61	  It is adjoint to the morphism
62	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
63	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
64	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
65	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
66	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
67	\end{definition}
68	
69	\section{The affine case}
70	
71	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for quasi-coherent sheaf morphisms}
72	
73	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
74	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
75	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
76	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
77	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
78	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
79	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
80	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
81	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
82	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
83	
84	\begin{lemma}\leanok
85	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
86	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
87	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
88	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
89	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
90	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
91	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
92	\end{lemma}
93	
94	\begin{proof}
95	
96	
97	
98	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
99	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
100	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
101	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
102	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
103	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
104	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
105	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
106	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
107	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
108	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
109	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
110	\end{proof}
111	
112	\begin{lemma}\leanok
113	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
114	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
115	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
116	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
117	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
118	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
119	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
120	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
121	  isomorphism.
122	\end{lemma}
123	
124	\begin{proof}
125	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
126	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
127	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
128	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
129	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
130	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
131	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
132	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
133	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
134	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
135	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
136	\end{proof}
137	
138	\begin{lemma}\leanok
139	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
140	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
142	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
143	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
144	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
145	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
146	\end{lemma}
147	
148	\begin{proof}
149	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
150	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
151	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
152	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
153	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
154	  affine opens.
155	\end{proof}
156	
157	\subsection{The affine computation}
158	
159	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
160	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
161	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
162	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
163	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
164	
165	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
166	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
167	their proof sketches.
168	
169	\begin{lemma}
170	
171	\leanok
172	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
173	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
175	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
176	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
177	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
178	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
179	  \[
180	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
181	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
182	  \]
183	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
184	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
185	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
186	\end{lemma}
187	
188	\begin{proof}
189	
190	
191	
192	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
193	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
194	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
195	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
196	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
197	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
198	\end{proof}
199	
200	\begin{lemma}
201	
202	\leanok
203	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
204	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
206	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
207	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
208	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
209	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
210	  \[
211	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
212	    \;\cong\;
213	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
214	  \]
215	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
216	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
217	  of \(N\).
218	\end{lemma}
219	
220	\begin{proof}
221	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
222	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
223	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
224	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
225	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
226	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
227	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
228	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
229	  equation of
230	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
231	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
232	\end{proof}

234	\begin{lemma}
235	
236	\leanok
237	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
238	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
240	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
241	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
242	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
243	  \[
244	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \widetilde{M}\bigr)
245	    \;\cong\;
246	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M .
247	  \]
248	\end{lemma}
249	
250	\begin{proof}
251	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
252	  Specialise Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} to \(N = \widetilde{M}\) and
253	  compose with the tilde-unit isomorphism
254	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{M}) \cong M\) of \(R'\)-modules (which restricts to an
255	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules after applying \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi\)).
256	\end{proof}
257	
258	\begin{lemma}
259	
260	\leanok
261	[Sections of an affine pushforward over an arbitrary open]
262	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
264	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
265	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, let \(N\) be
266	  an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module, and let \(U\) be an open of
267	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), with preimage
268	  \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(U)\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\). Then
269	  there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
270	  \[
271	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)
272	    \;\cong\;
273	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V)\bigr) .
274	  \]
275	  This is the open-indexed generalization of
276	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, which is the case \(U = \top\) (so that
277	  \(V = \top\) as well). The family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of these isomorphisms is moreover
278	  natural in the open argument: the structure-sheaf restriction maps on the two
279	  sides commute with the isomorphisms, so the family is a natural isomorphism of the
280	  two presheaves of \(R\)-modules in the open variable (this naturality is
281	  established in the proof below). For \(U = D(a)\) it is exactly the linear
282	  equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement~(1) in the proof of
283	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.

285	
286	\begin{proof}
287	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
288	  The construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim,
289	  with the top open \(\top\) replaced throughout by the arbitrary open \(U\) (and
290	  its preimage \(V\)). The point that makes this replacement free is that the
291	  \(R\)-module structure on the sections is supplied \emph{uniformly} by
292	  restriction of scalars along the global-sections ring map at the top open,
293	  independently of the evaluation open; consequently both sides unwind to the same
294	  nested restriction-of-scalars towers, reconciled by applying the
295	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the
296	  \(\top\)-level ring equation of
297	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. Naturality in the open
298	  follows because every constituent of the construction is either a structure-sheaf
299	  restriction map or conjugation by a fixed ring map, and all of these commute with
300	  further restriction along an inclusion of opens.
301	
302	  \medskip
303	  \emph{Naturality of the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) in the open (remark).} The family is
304	  in fact a natural isomorphism between two presheaves of \(R\)-modules on
305	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\): the source presheaf
306	  \(U \mapsto \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)\), with the
307	  structure-sheaf restriction maps of the pushforward, and the target presheaf
308	  \(U \mapsto
309	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U)\bigr)\),
310	  with the structure-sheaf restriction maps of \(N\) transported through the preimage
311	  operation \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) and read as \(R\)-linear maps by
312	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). Concretely, for any inclusion of opens
313	  \(U' \subseteq U\), with \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U\) and
314	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U'\), the square with horizontal maps
315	  \(e_U, e_{U'}\) and vertical maps the respective structure-sheaf restrictions
316	  commutes. The reason is structural: each \(e_U\) is a composite whose constituents
317	  are of exactly two kinds — structure-sheaf restriction maps (of \(N\),
318	  respectively of \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\)) and identity-on-carrier
319	  \(\operatorname{restrictScalars}\) repackagings (conjugation by the \emph{fixed},
320	  open-independent ring map supplied by the restriction-of-scalars reconciliation and
321	  by the \(\top\)-level ring equation of
322	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}). Restriction maps commute
323	  with further restriction by the presheaf functoriality axiom (using
324	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U' \subseteq
325	  (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U = V\), as \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) is
326	  monotone), and conjugation by a fixed ring map commutes with restriction because the
327	  ring map does not depend on \(U\); the naturality square is the paste of these
328	  component squares, and each \(e_U\) being an isomorphism makes the family a natural
329	  isomorphism. This open-naturality is exactly the compatibility invoked, at the
330	  inclusion \(D(a) \subseteq \top\), in the proof of
331	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
332	\end{proof}

352

370	\begin{lemma}

372	\leanok
373	[Restriction of scalars of a localized module]
374	  \label{lem:powers_restrictScalars}
375	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars}
376	  Let \(A\) be a commutative \(R\)-algebra, \(S \subseteq R\) a submonoid, and
377	  \(f : M \to N\) an \(A\)-linear map that exhibits \(N\) as the localization of
378	  \(M\) at the image submonoid

389	  submonoid \(S\) of \(R\) remains a localization after enlarging the base to an
390	  \(R\)-algebra \(A\) along the image submonoid \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\). One
391	  checks the three defining conditions of a localized module directly. Each

406	\begin{lemma}
407	
408	\leanok
409	[Section-level counit isomorphism from a localization hypothesis]
410	  \label{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
411	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.fromTildeΓ_app_isIso_of_isLocalizedModule}
412	  Let \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-module and \(a \in R\). Suppose the
413	  structure-sheaf restriction map \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\), read as
414	  an \(R\)-linear map of the global-section modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as
415	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then the
416	  tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit
417	  \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N : \widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)} \to N\) restricts to an
418	  isomorphism on the sections over the basic open \(D(a)\).

422	
423	
424	
425	  Over \(D(a)\) the section map \(L\) of the counit sits in the triangle identity
426	  \(L \circ j = \rho\), where
427	  \(j : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma\bigl(\widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)}, D(a)\bigr)\) is
428	  the tilde-localization map and \(\rho : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) is

437	
438	\begin{lemma}
439	
440	\leanok
441	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module, conditional form]
442	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional}
443	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_of_isLocalizedModule}
444	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
445	  lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
446	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and \(M\) an
447	  \(R'\)-module. Suppose that for every \(a \in R\) the structure-sheaf restriction
448	  of the pushforward \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) exhibits its
449	  sections over \(D(a)\) as the localization of its global sections at
450	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
451	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
452	  \[
453	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M \;\cong\;
454	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)} .
455	  \]
456	\end{lemma}

696	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
697	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
698	  \begin{enumerate}
699	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
700	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
701	      \(R\)-module of sections of
702	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
703	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
704	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
705	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
706	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
707	      closed under isomorphism; hence
708	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
709	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
710	      needed for the affine lane.
711	  \end{enumerate}
712	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
713	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
714	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
715	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
716	\end{remark}

1243	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1244	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1245	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1246	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1247	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1248	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1249	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1250	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1251	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1252	\end{lemma}
1253	\begin{proof}
1254	
1255	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1256	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1257	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1258	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using

1305	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1306	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\) an
1307	  \(A\)-module. There is a bundled \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
1308	  \[
1309	    \operatorname{regroup} :
1310	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
1311	  \]

1313	  \((r' \otimes 1) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\); equivalently its inverse sends

1315	  hypothesis.

1318	\begin{proof}
1319	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1320	  The underlying \(R'\)-linear equivalence is supplied, already as a bundled
1321	  \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over \(R'\), by the natively \(R'\)-linear
1322	  pushout-cancellation equivalence of
1323	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}. Crucially, that helper bundles
1324	  \(R'\)-linearity \emph{internally}: there is no hand-written scalar-compatibility
1325	  obligation, no induction over elementary tensors, and in particular \emph{no} residual
1326	  \(r' \cdot 0 = 0\) bookkeeping branch. The only work left to this proof is therefore to
1327	  promote that \(R'\)-linear equivalence to a morphism of \(R'\)-module \emph{objects} in
1328	  the exact extension/restriction-of-scalars packaging that the codomain and domain reads
1329	  produce. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1330	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1331	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1332	
1333	  \emph{The lone residual obstruction is the \(A\)-action diamond, resolved at the object
1334	  level.} The pushout-cancellation helper takes its relative tensor product over the
1335	  \emph{canonical} \(A\)-algebra structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1336	  (multiplication in the left factor), whereas the carrier object produced by the
1337	  dictionaries,
1338	  the extension of scalars of \(M\) along the left inclusion \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\),
1339	  tensors over the \(A\)-module structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\) induced by \(\iota\). These
1340	  two \(A\)-module structures on
1341	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) agree on elements but are distinct as data, so the two relative
1342	  tensor products are distinct types even though their underlying abelian groups and
1343	  their \(R'\)-actions coincide. This diamond is the \emph{only} thing the helper does
1344	  not already discharge, and — because the \(R'\)-linearity is now entirely internal to
1345	  the Mathlib equivalence — it is resolved purely at the carrier level by an
1346	  identity-on-elements \(R'\)-linear bridge
1347	  \[
1348	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M
1349	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1350	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M ,
1351	  \]
1352	  the identity map on generators, which is \(R'\)-linear because both sides carry the
1353	  same \(R'\)-action (restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\)).
1354	
1355	  Composing the bridge \(eT\) with the natively \(R'\)-linear helper equivalence and
1356	  promoting to an isomorphism of module objects yields the desired bundled
1357	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) objects. Being a composite
1358	  of linear equivalences it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents
1359	  carries a flatness hypothesis.
1360	\end{proof}

% --- Seam 2, step (iii) FINE re-break (iter-035): the conjugate-side discharge of the
%     leg-reindex crux, atomised into one mathematical claim per lemma. The prior coarse

\begin{lemma}[Conjugation distributes over composition (reassoc simp lemma)]
  \label{lem:conjugateEquiv_comp_mathlib}
  \lean{CategoryTheory.conjugateEquiv_comp}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1 : L_1 \dashv R_1\), \(\mathrm{adj}_2 : L_2 \dashv R_2\),
  \(\mathrm{adj}_3 : L_3 \dashv R_3\) be adjunctions between the same pair of categories, and let
  \(\alpha : L_2 \Rightarrow L_1\), \(\beta : L_3 \Rightarrow L_2\) be transformations of left adjoints.
  Then the conjugates compose, with order reversed onto the right-adjoint side:
  \[
    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_2)\,\alpha \;\circ\;
    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_2,\mathrm{adj}_3)\,\beta
      \;=\; \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_3)\,(\beta \circ \alpha) .
  \]
  Tagged \(\mathtt{@[reassoc (attr := simp)]}\), it is the lead member of the conjugate
  reassociation simp set: it fuses the per-adjunction conjugates of a composite adjunction into the
  conjugate of the composite (the conjugate-side form of the paste-of-mates).
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Inverse conjugation distributes over composition (reassoc simp lemma)]
  \label{lem:conjugateEquiv_symm_comp_mathlib}
  \lean{CategoryTheory.conjugateEquiv_symm_comp}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
  The inverse-direction companion of Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_comp_mathlib}: for transformations
  \(\alpha : R_1 \Rightarrow R_2\), \(\beta : R_2 \Rightarrow R_3\) of the right adjoints,
  \[
    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_2,\mathrm{adj}_3)^{-1}\beta \;\circ\;
    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_2)^{-1}\alpha
      \;=\; \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_3)^{-1}(\alpha \circ \beta) ,
  \]
  also \(\mathtt{@[reassoc (attr := simp)]}\). Together these two close the reassociation bookkeeping of
  the conjugate-side discharge.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}
[(conj-1a) Codomain read assembled natively in the composite-conjugate calculus]
  \label{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs_conj}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs_conj}
  \uses{lem:pullbackComp_eq_leftAdjointCompIso, lem:leftAdjointCompIso_mathlib,
        lem:conjugateEquiv_leftAdjointCompIso_inv_mathlib, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso,
        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
  This is the codomain read of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs} \emph{rebuilt proof-free, natively in the
  composite-conjugate calculus}. Fix \(\psi : R \to R'\), \(\varphi : R \to A\) and an \(A\)-module
  \(M\); let \(e : X' \xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Spec}(A \otimes_R R')\) be the canonical square
  isomorphism (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackSpecIso_mathlib}) and \(\iota_A : A \to A \otimes_R R'\),
  \(\iota_{R'} : R' \to A \otimes_R R'\) the tensor inclusions. Reading the codomain
  \(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\) along the \emph{explicit composite} legs
  \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\), \(f' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_{R'}\), the
  comparison isomorphism of \(R'\)-modules
  \[
    \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}^{\sharp} :
    \Gamma\bigl(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\bigr)
    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M
  \]
  is assembled with its pullback factor taken as the composite-adjunction comparison
  \(\operatorname{leftAdjointCompIso}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:leftAdjointCompIso_mathlib}) of the two free
  morphisms \(e\), \(\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\) — the pasted square entering through the pushforward
  congruence \(\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\) and composition \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}\) coherences —
  rather than as the project's \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}\); the two agree by
  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackComp_eq_leftAdjointCompIso}, so this read carries \emph{no} leg-equality data
  \(\mathit{hfst}/\mathit{hsnd}\). It is then composed with the affine pullback
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}) and pushforward (Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso})
  dictionaries. Because the comparison object is built from the free morphisms, its conjugate inverts to
  the right-adjoint comparison in one step
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_leftAdjointCompIso_inv_mathlib}).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:pullbackComp_eq_leftAdjointCompIso, lem:leftAdjointCompIso_mathlib,
        lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
  Copy the assembly of Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs} verbatim, with the single
  change that the pullback-composition factor \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A}\) is
  replaced by the abstract composite-adjunction comparison
  \(\operatorname{leftAdjointCompIso}(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})\). The
  replacement is along the identity of isomorphisms
  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackComp_eq_leftAdjointCompIso}, so the resulting object isomorphism is the same
  data presented natively in the conjugate calculus and carries no propositional leg-equality.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[(conj-1b) The conjugate-native read agrees with the concrete read]
  \label{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs_conj_eq}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs_conj_eq}
  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs_conj, lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs,
        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}
  The conjugate-native codomain read \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}^{\sharp}\)
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs_conj}) equals, as an isomorphism of
  \(R'\)-modules, the leg-parametrised read \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}^{\flat}\)
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs}) and hence, at the literal projection legs, the
  concrete read \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}). This single
  bridge keeps the downstream consumers of the concrete read — in particular the green reduction of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex} — defeq-unchanged when the reindex crux is re-expressed
  against the conjugate-native object.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs_conj, lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs}
  The only difference between the two assemblies is the pullback factor, which is
  \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A}\) in the leg-parametrised read and
  \(\operatorname{leftAdjointCompIso}(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})\) in the
  conjugate-native read; these are equal by
  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackComp_eq_leftAdjointCompIso}. Substituting that equality identifies the two
  composites; the agreement with the concrete read at the projection legs is the cone-leg factorization
  already recorded for Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs}.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[(conj-2b) The pullback-side leg of the conjugate identity]
  \label{lem:base_change_mate_reindex_conj_pullbackLeg}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_reindex_conj_pullbackLeg}
  \uses{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib,
        lem:conjugateEquiv_leftAdjointCompIso_inv_mathlib, lem:pullbackComp_eq_leftAdjointCompIso}
  In the conjugate-side discharge of the leg-reindex (after
  \(\operatorname{conj}^{-1}\) has been applied to both sides, Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateIsoEquiv_mathlib}),
  the pullback factor is identified in one step. The conjugate of the inverse of the composite
  comparison \(\operatorname{leftAdjointCompIso}\) carried by the conjugate-native read
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs_conj}) is, by
  Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_leftAdjointCompIso_inv_mathlib}, the right-adjoint square isomorphism
  \(e\); and the conjugate of the inverse pullback-composition coherence
  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{inv}}\) is the pushforward
  composition coherence \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{e,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) by
  Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib} (the two coherences being interchangeable on
  the comparison object by Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackComp_eq_leftAdjointCompIso}). Thus the pullback-side
  leg of the conjugate identity is the pushforward coherence, with no propositional transport.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib,
        lem:conjugateEquiv_leftAdjointCompIso_inv_mathlib, lem:pullbackComp_eq_leftAdjointCompIso}
  Both identifications are direct applications of the cited conjugation identities to the free legs
  \(e\), \(\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\); the interchange of \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}\) and
  \(\operatorname{leftAdjointCompIso}\) is Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackComp_eq_leftAdjointCompIso}.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[(conj-2c) The pushforward-side collapse of the conjugate identity]
  \label{lem:base_change_mate_reindex_conj_pushforwardCollapse}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_reindex_conj_pushforwardCollapse}
  \uses{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id, lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_inv_eq_id,
        lem:gammaMap_pushforwardCongr_hom}
  On the pushforward side of the conjugate identity, read on global sections over
  \(\operatorname{Spec} R\), the transparent coherences collapse: the two
  pushforward-composition coherences
  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp})^{\mathrm{hom}}\), \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp})^{\mathrm{inv}}\) and the
  pushforward-congruence coherence \((\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\,\mathit{comm})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) all have
  identity \(\Gamma\)-image (Lemmas~\ref{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id},
  \ref{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_inv_eq_id}, \ref{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardCongr_hom}). Hence these
  three factors of the inner composite drop out, leaving only the unit factor to be evaluated.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id, lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_inv_eq_id,
        lem:gammaMap_pushforwardCongr_hom}
  Each of the three coherence components is transparent on sections — its value at every open is the
  identity or a presheaf transport — so its image under \(\Gamma = \Gamma_R\) is the identity by the
  three collapse lemmas cited. A composite is unchanged by deleting identity factors.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[(conj-2d) Cross-layer transport of the affine unit through the pushforward $\Gamma$-comparison]
  \label{lem:base_change_mate_reindex_conj_crossLayer}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_reindex_conj_crossLayer}
  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value, lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_symm_mathlib,
        lem:conjugateEquiv_comp_mathlib, lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
  This is the historical bottom of the term-mode route, isolated here as a single conjugate-side claim.
  The surviving \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)\)-unit factor of the inner composite must be transported
  across the codomain read's \(\mathrm{unit\_iso}^{-1}\) factor, which lives one functor layer up,
  through the pushforward \(\Gamma\)-comparison \(\gamma_\psi\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}).
  The claim is that this cross-layer transport is effected by the transposed unit-across-conjugate
  coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_symm_mathlib}, composed one functor layer up via the
  conjugation-composition coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_comp_mathlib} (the Seam~1 tool raised
  one layer), and that the lone surviving affine unit then evaluates by Seam~1
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}) to the inner value
  \(\rho : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\). The point is that the naturality of \(\gamma_\psi\)
  enters \emph{as a conjugate-component identity}, never as a positional naturality rewrite under the
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module instance diamond.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value, lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_symm_mathlib,
        lem:conjugateEquiv_comp_mathlib}
  Apply the transposed unit-across-conjugate coherence
  Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_symm_mathlib} to the right-adjoint transformation
  \(\gamma_\psi\)-naturality factor, composed through the conjugation-composition coherence
  Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_comp_mathlib} to raise it past the
  \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\) layer (exactly the Seam~1 mechanism, one functor layer up). This
  cancels the codomain read's \(\mathrm{unit\_iso}^{-1}\), leaving the bare affine
  \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)\)-unit, whose value through the tilde dictionaries is the algebraic unit
  \(\rho\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[(conj-2a) The leg-reindex coherence on the conjugate side]
  \label{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs_conj}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs_conj}
  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs_conj, lem:conjugateIsoEquiv_mathlib,
        lem:iterated_mateEquiv_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:conjugateEquiv_comp_mathlib,
        lem:conjugateEquiv_symm_comp_mathlib, lem:base_change_mate_reindex_conj_pullbackLeg,
        lem:base_change_mate_reindex_conj_pushforwardCollapse,
        lem:base_change_mate_reindex_conj_crossLayer, def:base_change_mate_inner_value}
  This is the leg-reindex coherence of Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs}, restated at
  the explicit composite legs \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\),
  \(f' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_{R'}\) and \emph{against the conjugate-native codomain read}
  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}^{\sharp}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs_conj}): the
  inner composite \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\), read on global sections over \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) with
  that codomain, equals the canonical \(R\)-linear map
  \(\rho : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) of Definition~\ref{def:base_change_mate_inner_value}.
  Both sides are values of the conjugation bijection (Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateIsoEquiv_mathlib};
  abstractly, the Beck--Chevalley reformulation Lemma~\ref{lem:iterated_mateEquiv_conjugateEquiv_mathlib}
  certifies that the comparison is a conjugate), so it suffices to prove the equality of their conjugate
  preimages on the right-adjoint side.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs_conj, lem:conjugateIsoEquiv_mathlib,
        lem:conjugateEquiv_comp_mathlib, lem:conjugateEquiv_symm_comp_mathlib,
        lem:base_change_mate_reindex_conj_pullbackLeg,
        lem:base_change_mate_reindex_conj_pushforwardCollapse,
        lem:base_change_mate_reindex_conj_crossLayer}
  Apply \((\operatorname{conj})^{-1}\) to both sides (injectivity of the conjugation bijection,
  Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateIsoEquiv_mathlib}), and lift every locked right-adjoint-side natural
  transformation to a free left-adjoint-side preimage by surjectivity of the same bijection, so that no
  diamond-locked literal remains. The resulting identity is then closed by combining the three isolated
  legs: the pullback-side identification
  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_reindex_conj_pullbackLeg}, the pushforward-side collapse
  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_reindex_conj_pushforwardCollapse}, and the cross-layer transport
  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_reindex_conj_crossLayer}, with the reassociation handled by the
  conjugate simp set (Lemmas~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_comp_mathlib},
  \ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_symm_comp_mathlib}; together with the conjugation whiskering and associator
  coherences of the same Mathlib family). The surviving affine unit evaluates to \(\rho\) inside the
  cross-layer leg.
\end{proof}
